title: Monte-Carlo Simulation of a Single Tiered Supply Chain with Total Cost Minimization Objective
summary: Built an award-winning simulation study that used VBA-driven Monte Carlo experimentation and route-cost evaluation to identify cost-minimal supplier-assembler-demand network configurations.
highlights: Won first place in the Simulation Competition at the IEOM Society's November 2024 conference.
Executed 10,000-plus Monte Carlo simulations in Excel Macros VBA to search for cost-minimal routing decisions.
Integrated Google Maps API distance calculations to make transportation cost estimates reflect real route distances.

sections.heading: Competition framing
body: The work was structured as a simulation competition problem around total-cost minimization in a single-tier supply chain. The challenge was not just building a simulation, but making the simulation useful for decision-making under uncertainty.
That meant linking random variation in the network to a clear optimization target instead of stopping at descriptive experimentation.

sections.heading: Simulation engine
body: The core engine was built in Excel Macros VBA and used to run more than 10,000 Monte Carlo trials. This made it possible to explore a large configuration space while preserving an environment that was easy to communicate and test.
Each run contributed to identifying routing patterns that lowered aggregate supply chain cost under the modeled assumptions.

sections.heading: Cost realism
body: A key improvement was the use of Google Maps API distance data rather than abstract or assumed travel values. That grounded the transportation-cost layer in route lengths closer to operational reality.
The result was a simulation that did more than produce theoretical minima: it translated geography into the optimization logic.

sections.heading: Outcome
body: The project was recognized with a first-place award, which indicates the work stood out not only technically but also in how clearly the optimization logic was expressed through simulation.
It also marked an early bridge between operations analytics and the later supply-chain-focused work in graduate study.
